data.title: Salesforce: Billions Of Reasons To Own
data.description:
Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) ripped higher as the market appreciated the large share buyback, but the SaaSpocalypse fears have returned. The leading CRM solution continues reporting strong growth with impressive cash flows while the stock trades at multi-year lows. My investment thesis is bullish on the opportunity to dip-buy a leading software play alongside a massive share buyback.

Massive Stock Buyback

Salesforce announced major plans to repurchase $50 billion worth of shares along with the FQ4'26 results. The stock only has a market cap of around $170 billion now, so the buyback is significant to the valuation.

Back on March 11, Salesforce announced a $25 billion accelerated share repurchase, ASR, agreement. The company completed the prepayment of the $25 billion via a debt offering, and Salesforce already took initial delivery of 103 million shares, ~80% of the shares to be repurchased under this ASR and fully settled by FQ3'27.

The CFO made the following statement about the share repurchase:

This $25 billion ASR reflects our increased conviction in the durability of our growth and cash flow trajectory.

The market doesn't like that the company went to market to borrow $25 billion in senior notes to cover the up-front ASR. Salesforce had the option to repay debt over time or repurchase shares over time, and the company saw the current price as too attractive to pass up.

Salesforce ended FY26 with a cash balance of $9.6 billion and $14.4 billion in debt. The company had a strong balance sheet entering the share repurchase program.

Salesforce generated $14.4 billion in free cash flow last year. The forecast is for up to 10% cash flow growth in FY27 for a free cash flow target in the $16 billion range. In essence, the software company has the cash on hand and cash flow in FY27 to cover the massive ASR.

The company already had a strong net payout yield entering the ASR at 7.8%, but this $25 billion buyback took the metric to the next level. The NPY measures the combined dividend yield and net share buyback yields versus the market cap, with Salesforce already having a strong buyback combined with a minimal 1% dividend yield.

The new NPY will shoot to 15% based on this ASR alone and much higher depending on how Salesforce handles the additional $25 billion share buyback approval. Considering the balance sheet will suddenly have nearly $40 billion in debt, the market clearly prefers a slow process to additional buybacks, with most free cash flow going to repaying debt, not share buybacks for now.

For now, the NPY will hit ~21% due to Salesforce repurchasing a net $9.5 billion worth of shares in the prior 3 quarters. When combined with the $25 billion ASR, the company will have a TTM share buyback of $34.5 billion, or ~20%, while the dividend yield adds another 1% to the yield.

SaaSpocalypse

Salesforce continues to report strong growth for a company with nearly $40 billion in software sales. The company reported FQ4 sales grew 12.1% and guided to FY27 growth topping 10% and reaching towards 11%.

The stock trades at only 14x the FY27 consensus analyst EPS targets of $13.20, which doesn't appear to even factor in a reduction in the share count topping 10% already. Hence, the attractiveness of management loading up on share buybacks while the stock is cheap, trading at up to half of the forward P/E multiple of the last couple of years.

The biggest risk to the story is AI eating software businesses. The big pushback is that enterprises aren't going to trust their customer relations systems used by sales forces to a Vibe coding process that doesn't provide a complete enterprise level of security and support.

New AI startups could eat into the business, so investors clearly want Salesforce to quickly cut the debt level to reduce any balance sheet risk and the interest expense from a high debt level. Ultimately, the company will lack the financial flexibility to address any future opportunities unless debt is quickly repaid.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Salesforce is retesting the recent lows around $180. Investors should use any successful test as an opportunity to start loading up on the leading CRM business, with AI fears overplayed and the company aggressively buying cheap shares to boost earnings. I rate CRM a "Strong Buy."

data.title: RTX Corporation (RTX) Stock Analysis: 36 Years Of Dividends Vs. Quant "Hold" | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2 Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain, but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. Today’s episode is from this request. So, let's throw 2minutes on the clock and dive-in.

Today, we are looking at RTX Corporation, ticker symbol RTX. Kicking things off here with the Quant rating system, it is currently a Hold rating on the stock, and we're going to dive into the metrics here in just a moment. Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha Analysts, they are currently a Buy in aggregate on this stock, and that's from 9 analysts providing their coverage in the last 90-days.

Lastly, Wall Street analysts are currently a Buy on this stock, and that's 22 analysts providing their coverage in the last 90-days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha Analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

Now, let's dive deeper. This is a $266.72 billion market capitalization company found within the industrial sector and the aerospace and defense industry. Kicking things off here, the Valuation grade is currently a D-. If we look at that PEG non-GAAP forward it is 2.87, compared to the sector at just 1.57, showing that it's a bit richly valuated here. In addition, the trailing 12-months price-to-book ratio is 4.08, compared to the sector at 3.03.

Jumping into the Growth grade, it is currently a C- with the revenue growth year-over-year of 9.74%, compared to the sector at just 4.65%, as well as the forward revenue growth number here of 7.26%, compared to the sector at 6% flat. In addition, the EPS diluted growth year-over-year is 39.72%, compared to the sector at 6.93%.

Jumping over to Profitability, it is currently an A+ grade. That net income margin of 7.60% is above the sector median at 6.88%. And cash from operations is coming in at $10.57 billion, compared to the sector at just $406.9 million.

Jumping into Momentum, it is currently an A- grade. One year price performance of 47.36% beats the sector at 9.66%. And even more recently on the three month price performance, 8.87% compared to the sector which is down 3.94%.

Lastly, jumping into the Revisions, currently a B grade. That's 12 up revisions and 7 down revisions for earnings per share over the last 3 months and 16 up revisions and 4 down revisions for revenue numbers over the last 3 months.

Now, RTX does pay a small dividend of 1.37% forward, but look at these grades here. Safety is an A-. The growth grade is a B-. The yield is a C-, and consistency is an A+. If we jump into the consistency here you'll notice that they have been paying their dividend for 36 years and growing their dividend for the last 33 years.

So, what are you waiting for? Come over here to Seeking Alpha. Follow RTX Corporation. Put it into a watch list. Get all those free breaking news alerts. And that's going to wrap it up for this episode. data.title: Pay To Play In The Strait Of Hormuz
data.description:
The fate of the Strait of Hormuz is the most important consideration for much of the world right now, given how much oil transits through it—1 in 5 barrels globally. Since the outbreak of the U.S.-Iran War just under a month ago, transit has been perilous, with civilian vessels having been hit by the Iranian military.

Now that oil is really starting to become thin around certain parts of the world, like Japan and Asia at large, Tehran has put out a new policy: transit is possible for non-American and Israeli ships, but it comes with a catch: you have to pay the toll, and the toll is $2M, preferably in yuan or crypto.

What at first appeared to me as a rogue IRGC commander trying to profit from the situation is now being considered by the Iranian parliament, which may codify a toll for ships passing through Hormuz.

Hormuz Isn't Like The Suez Or Panama Canals

One of the reasons why countries can take fees on transit through tight corridors (which was the justification for Iran imposing fees) is because the vessels have to actually enter the country to transit. But the Strait of Hormuz doesn't exist inside any country's physical boundaries, and the part where ships transit actually sits inside Oman's exclusive economic zone.

Even then, UNCLOS, international maritime law, holds that straits cannot be monetized like canals. Coastal nations are unable to do what Iran is trying to do, at least legally.

But international maritime law obviously does not matter here. What matters is who will pay the illegal toll. That figure is still lost in the fog of war, and it's unclear how many ships, if any at all, have actually paid Iran.

One sign that we can use to confirm this is happening is that ships that have passed through the strait—before these new 10 just announced, as those were a “present to Trump,” as said by the IRGC—have been rerouting themselves close to the Iranian coastline. This is presumably so that the IRGC, whose projection off the coastline is limited and who reportedly do not have much apart from satellite communications tools, no radar, etc., can verify the identity of the ship before allowing passage.

How long this will go on for is unclear, but this exact route has been taken by several tankers now.

The U.S. Could End This Soon

It's doubtful from my view that the U.S. and its Gulf allies will allow this kind of extortion, especially since it will be directly impacting the major exporters in the region like Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

For any toll to be respected, there must be enough force to back it up. It's unclear if Iran could actually enforce this, or for how long, especially if the U.S. military came to interdict in the islands in the strait. I expect that is the plan with the Marine expeditionary unit and paratroopers recently routed to the Middle East, not Kharg Island. For reference, I'm talking about the two little islands between which the Pakistani oil tanker made its route in the above image.

Holding Hormuz (north) and Larak islands would be a much easier feat than holding onto Kharg, much farther into the strait. They are largely uninhabited compared to the other islands and give the U.S. a highly strategic outpost to ensure safe passage of ships through the normal shipping lanes and intercept any Iranian attacks.

The Economic Implications Of The Toll

The only future in which I see this toll becoming a more permanent fixture of the strait is if it is agreed to as part of a peace deal in exchange for other concessions. However, I find that incredibly doubtful.

For now, the economic implications are minimal, but if this toll becomes long-term, consider that the economic ramifications could snowball and get unruly quickly. The $2M amount is just too high for anything but ransom negotiations.

Some perspective is needed here on what $2M actually means for a tanker passing through the strait. Consider that a VLCC, the largest and most common tanker moving oil out of the strait, transports ~2M barrels. At the current crisis pricing for oil, $100, the toll is 1% of cargo value. But at the price oil was at a month ago, $67, this represents a far greater share.

Tanker operations are unlikely to be keen on paying this, as it would eat into their margins at ludicrous rates. Consider that Suez's highest rate is $250,000/transit, and Panama's is $300,000/transit. Though smaller ships with less cargo value transit here, the difference is stark.

Major shipping firms that operate VLCCs that transit the strait frequently, like Frontline Plc (FRO), do not have enough cash on hand or the ability to sustainably raise the cash to cover this kind of toll for their fleet.

They operate 42 VLCCs and could not cover their tolls for a given year with their cash-on-hand, assuming all other expenses stay the same. It would bankrupt them, and they are one of the stronger shipping firms. Smaller firms will go under quickly if this is the new norm (another reason I doubt this toll will become permanent).

There is a question about the near term for prices if the toll is getting paid to stave off shortages in Asian economies. I would expect the oil coming out of the Gulf currently to carry a premium even after escalation.

This means that the shock will be felt unevenly, and regionality will be on display; Asian economies like Japan, Korea, and Taiwan will likely face the worst of it, as they buy oil and natural gas from one of the most expensive sources currently. The UAE and Qatar are major exporters to the region, and both have crude fields sitting at over $150/barrel. These countries have major exposure to these prices; for example, a Japanese firm, INPEX (IPXHY), has a 12% stake in the Upper Zakum field.

Note: You can find these prices here.

Contrast that to U.S.-born crude, which sells for far less. There are blends in the U.S. going for as low as $74/barrel. Firms in the U.S. are not paying nearly the same as Asian firms for oil currently. Similar prices apply for Mexican and South American crude, and so the Western Hemisphere is operating on a premium, but nothing like the East is.

I would expect this divergence to continue for the remainder of the conflict but to stay in place after if the Iranian toll is stickier than I think it will be. That would structurally change the market and could essentially codify a premium for Gulf oil over Western oil.

That could lead to inflation for nations like Japan, Korea, and Taiwan as energy prices stay elevated in the long term and would likely bring even more demand for U.S. crude in the future. Again, I don't see that as likely, since I don't believe a toll will be allowed to remain in the strait.

One thing I will say is to be careful of speculating on derivatives of this trade, like oil freight rates. These have acted like a call option on conflict in the Middle East and are a benefit for shipping firms like FRO in the short term but damaging for the economy in the long term. Investors are speculating on these rates through funds like the BWET ETF. All I can say is that I think that ship has sailed—pun intended—and it's likely too late to jump into the trade. I would believe that we're at or near a peak for freight rates.

Conclusion

The Iranians are attempting to toll the ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, and rumors are that some ships are paying it. The Iranian government is attempting to implement the toll officially. Despite its international illegality, the reality is that this toll could become a part of some broader quid pro quo to establish peace and safe transit, though I find that doubtful.

If a toll remains in force for any long period of time, even if it is another month, it will weigh heavily on shipping firms. I expect it to exaggerate the current premium experienced by Gulf oil, and a long-term agreement of payment for transit would meaningfully decouple Eastern and Western oil prices on a more permanent basis. If that happens, I expect Eastern inflation to jump far faster than Western inflation when it comes to global oil prices and how they are affecting economies through higher gas and diesel costs, etc.

The fog of war is still very thick, and it's unclear how long negotiations will go on for, if the U.S. will deploy troops to take strategic outposts to ensure passage and break the toll, or what the fate of the war itself will be.

I hope for peace.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Best Stocks To Buy As Stagflation Fear Overshadows Growth Potential
data.description:
Stagflation Fears Present Contrarian Opportunities

Fears of stagflation have returned to financial media headlines as oil prices surge, and the one-time price distortions of tariffs are appearing in inflation reports. This backdrop has prompted investors to rotate away from certain sectors, such as Basic Materials (XLB), Consumer Discretionary (XLY), and Information Technology (XLK). However, today’s backdrop differs significantly from the 1970s. As Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell noted at last week’s FOMC press meeting, "When we use the term stagflation, I always have to point out, that was a 1970s term at a time when unemployment was in double figures, and inflation was really high, and the Misery Index was super high… And that's not the case right now."

'Misery Index' Still Less Than Half the 70s and 80s Peaks

A hint of how certain sectors that are either beaten down or held back from higher price appreciation appeared this week when headlines suggested war with Iran may be winding down. Basic Materials stocks, particularly miners, as well as Consumer Discretionary and certain Information Technology names, jumped higher on the news. For investors willing to lean contrarian while fear still pervades market sentiment, now can be an opportune time to buy high-quality, Quant Strong Buys that can rebound quickly as sentiment normalizes.

How I Selected the Best Stocks to Buy the Stagflation Fear

To select the five best stocks recently held back by stagflation fear, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and targeted Quant Strong Buys. Then I selected the Sector & Industry filter so I could sort for targeted sectors, including Basic Materials, Consumer Discretionary, and Information Technology.

1. Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE)

  • Market Capitalization: $18.46B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 9 out of 282

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 3 out of 49

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

As markets have plummeted, I recently wrote two articles to capture potential buy-the-dip opportunities:

  • Buy the Dip: Best Stocks With Forward EPS Growth Above 40%

  • Buy the Dip: Top Tech Stocks After The Pullback

Starting with what may be one of the best “buy the dip” opportunities in the market now, Coeur Mining has been caught in the crosscurrents of a stronger dollar and weaker market sentiment, which have beaten down miners, despite strong fundamentals. CDE has fallen more than 25% over the past month, but it bounced 5% higher this week through midday Wednesday, as Iran tensions and oil prices eased. The Magnificent Seven stocks, or "Lag 7," have fallen substantially behind the remaining 493 stocks in the S&P 500. As oil prices and inflation fears fade further, downward pressure on gold and silver prices can also lift, making miners with a strong combination of valuation and growth metrics, such as CDE, early beneficiaries.

CDE’s forward Non-GAAP P/E of 7.49 gives it a 52% discount to sector peers, and on a trailing basis, its 0.04 PEG suggests nearly an 84% discount after factoring in its growth. For some perspective, Coeur's Q4 earnings results reflected a period when the price of gold ranged from about $3850 to $4500 per troy ounce. Gold’s price, at the time of this writing, was about $4550, which suggests miners still enjoy similar support in Q1 2026. Shifting to Coeur’s forward growth, standout metrics are highlighted by forward revenue growth of 83.71%, which is over 15x the sector median. Underscoring the company’s operational efficiency, its forward EBITDA growth of over 138% and operating cash flow of 171% crush sector peers by similar margins.

With fears of stagnation likely overdone, CDE offers upside potential as investors rotate back into cyclicals and aggressive-growth areas, which sets the stage for my next rebound candidate.

2. Sandisk Corporation (SNDK)

  • Market Capitalization: $100.05B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 524

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 26

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage, and Peripherals

One of the holdings in my Pro Quant Portfolio, Sandisk, is a classic case of an AI growth story overshadowed by stagflationary fears. As a key player in flash storage and memory, the company is tied to data center growth and related infrastructure buildouts, which are trends that remain intact regardless of short-term inflation concerns. The recent weakness in tech reflects broad-based selling in reaction to higher Treasury yields and dollar strength, not a deterioration in fundamentals, which are rich for SanDisk, one of the most-attractive value+growth stories in the market today.

While many top-rated AI stocks have strong growth metrics, their valuations are high relative to the sector. However, SanDisk’s valuation metrics reveal attractive discounts. For example, it’s rare to see a stock that has appreciated over 1,100% in one year like Sandisk, but still has a forward Non-GAAP forward P/E of 16.83, offering nearly a 22% discount to the sector median. Even more impressive is the forward PEG 0.08, which represents a 94% discount to peers. The company’s PEG is so low because of its forward earnings growth, which is driven by extreme AI-related demand for high-performance storage. Only a few companies, which include Micron Technology (MU), one of Alpha Picks’ top holdings, are in such a fortunate position.

Consecutively topping earnings, SNDK reported a Q2 2026 EPS of $6.20, a $2.66 beat and revenue of $3.03 billion, up a massive 61.25% year-over-year. Sandisk is positioned as a primary beneficiary of an AI-fueled NAND flash supercycle, with gross margins exceeding 50% and expectations of further improvement. As investors return their focus to cyclical growth rather than macro noise, SNDK is well positioned to continue its upward trajectory, leading to another technology name with similar AI leverage.

3. Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE)

  • Market Capitalization: $57.26B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 3 out of 524

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 37

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

A standout in both my Alpha Picks and Pro Quant Portfolio stocks, Lumentum has also experienced downside pressure in recent months. However, its past momentum is showing signs of return as its exposure to optical networking and AI-related infrastructure growth is impossible for bullish investors to ignore. Demand for high-speed connectivity and data transmission continues to surge, driven by hyperscaler investment, which has yet to show sensitivity to short-term macro pressures. Recent earnings strength and attractive key valuation metrics prove its Quant Strong Buy worthiness.

LITE offers a case in point on why the PEG ratio matters more in a growth environment than the superficial P/E that many investors focus on. The company's 101.07 Non-GAAP forward P/E ratio appears as a red flag on the surface, explaining the 'F' for this metric in its Valuation Factor Grade scorecard. But a quick glance lower reveals the 0.77 PEG, which indicates more than a 40% discount to the sector median. This suggests that a continuation of growth leaves room for more price appreciation, especially as macro headline noise fades.

LITE’s forward EBITDA growth of nearly 133% is more than 8x the sector median’s 15.24%, while its forward EPS Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) supports. LITE has also strengthened its position as a strategic partner in providing critical laser supply following NVIDIA Corporation’s (NVDA) $2 billion investment in the company, and was recently announced as a new addition to the S&P 500 (SP 500), effective March 23. A recent announcement revealing a $2B quarterly revenue run rate reinforces its long-term growth potential. As stagflation fears fade and investors rotate back into high-growth technology, LITE stands to benefit, leading to a rebound story, this time tied to an overlooked consumer discretionary idea.

4. American Public Education, Inc. (APEI)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.08B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 2 out of 480

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 26

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Education Services

American Public Education has been swept up in market sentiment fears around consumer discretionary spending that can come with stagflation, despite its strong fundamentals in a segment with relatively stable demand. As a provider of online postsecondary education and workforce training, APEI benefits from forward growth potential that runs contrary to expectations during periods of economic uncertainty. This disconnect sets up an opportunity for investors to buy while the market looks the other way. This stock has its most attractive Quant Factors displayed in Growth and Revision grades, both of which show impressive improvement over the past six months.

While APEI’s year-over-year revenue growth of 3.89% scores just a little better than the sector median, its forward EBIT growth of 33.37% gives a better picture of its operating performance, which is more than 5x the sector median. At its recent Q4 quarterly earnings call, management provided an outlook expectation for continued enrollment momentum and margin expansion, outlining $685M-$695M 2026 revenue guidance. This forward momentum supports the company’s A+ Revisions Factor Grade, which is a predictive measure of price appreciation.

With improving fundamentals and increasing demand for its services, APEI is positioned for greater price appreciation as macro fears fade. A shift in sentiment can also benefit our final stock and opportunity tied to recovery strength.

5. DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.97B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 8 out of 169

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 2 out of 14

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

DiamondRock Hospitality has faced downside pressure amid concerns that higher inflation and gas prices could decrease travel demand. However, the upper-half of the so-called "K-economy" consumer spending remains resilient, and demand in leisure and group travel continues to hold up better than expected. The recent pullback is more a reflection of short-term macro-driven caution than a real deterioration in fundamentals. This sets up an opportunity to buy a consumer discretionary-related REIT with a strong valuation/growth setup, as illustrated by Quant Factor Grades.

A more telling ratio for an REIT is the forward P/FFO, which is 8.38, supporting a 35.62% discount to sector peers. This metric further supports its 'B-' valuation grade, indicating investors are paying less for each dollar of recurring cash flow.

DRH’s sector-leading 15.81% AFFO Growth (3Y Hist. CAGR), over 6x the sector median, indicates the REIT has a history of expanding its cash flow much faster than peers. The 81.71% Dividend Growth Rate 3Y (CAGR), which is more than 30x the sector median, demonstrates extreme confidence in future cash flows and a commitment to returning capital. DRH’s yield is a solid 4.24%. When stagflation fears subside and recession expectations ease, travel-related REITs like DRH can benefit as investors rotate back into cyclical areas. This positions DRH as another recovery play and rounds out my list of five stocks primed to benefit from improving sentiment.

Conclusion: Stagflation Fears Create Opportunities and Risk

In combination, these stocks highlight a broader theme in which investors may be overestimating the risk of stagflation while underappreciating the potential for a return to growth and cyclical demand. From AI-driven tech to global education and travel, each name has either been pulled lower or higher returns have been capped by fear that is probably overdone. While I have acknowledged conditions for mild stagflation, the 1970s environment is not likely to repeat. As fears tied to oil prices, tariffs, and associated inflation begin to fade, these dislocations can reverse quickly, as we saw in price jumps this week when hopes of an end to the war in Iran surfaced. That said, the economic and market environment remains fluid and unpredictable on a daily basis, and ripple effects from higher oil prices can persist for months after the conflict ends. In volatile markets, aggressive investors can be selective, while other investors may want to be more cautious.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'strong buy' quant stocks. Members can check out our new Alpha Picks app in the App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy Investing!

data.title: BIZD Vs. PBDC: Why I'm Downgrading To 'Sell' As The Yield Trap Closes
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In spite of the fundamental headwinds casting a shadow over the BDC sector, a lot of investors continue to maintain their confidence in these assets. But I see more significant risks, and the risk profile is starting to outweigh the double-digit returns of funds like the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) and the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC). Some of the most notable developments have centered on Blue Owl Capital, Inc. (OWL), which has seen its stock lose more than 40% since the start of 2026. The freeze on redemptions by Blue Owl is causing panic among retail and institutional investors that previously considered alternative lending one of the safest market segments for generating high passive income. Century Capital's financial irregularities are exacerbating the situation, and Moody's has downgraded FS KKR Capital (FSK) to «junk» status (Ba1). This company's non-performing loan ratio has reached 5.5%, with the software crash in Q1 2026 increasingly hitting software companies, who have historically been some of BDC’s key borrowers. Considering that BDCs must repay or refinance $12.7 billion in unsecured debt in 2026, the impact on NII and future payments will be even more severe. This article aims not merely to provide a comparative analysis of BIZD and PBDC rather to justify why I am changing my rating on them from «Hold» to «Sell.»

Previous Thesis

A key point of my previous article on BIZD and PBDC was how focusing on active portfolio management for PBDC would yield higher returns than BIZD. Furthermore, it is PBDC that has demonstrated higher total return metrics over time, showing the early effectiveness of active management through portfolio rebalancing and the elimination of BDC assets with poor performance. Also, it was my opinion that the active management style of PBDC’s portfolio managers would enable them to anticipate the potential negative impact of continued monetary easing by the Fed by shifting to BDCs with a higher proportion of fixed-rate loans. Since the publication of my article, total returns for BIZD have been -10.67%, while PBDC returns have been -10.91%. Therefore, the active management of PBDC did not yield any additional benefits. I will discuss the reasons for this in a new article.

Forecast for Fed Interest Rates and Its Impact on BDC’s Outlook for 2026–2027

The current developments in the Middle East are increasing inflationary risks. Short-term inflation expectations, however, have risen to 3.3%, while long-term expectations remain unchanged at 2.4%. Reports that Iran refuses to engage in negotiations because of a lack of trust in the U.S. government suggest that we are unlikely to see a de-escalation of the conflict in the coming weeks. Capital is increasingly moving into cash, while US Treasury bond yields are rising.

Earlier (prior to the events in the Middle East), markets had expected the Fed to cut rates by 50 basis points in 2026. As of now, the rate forecasts have changed dramatically. As an example, participants in the market do not expect the next rate cut of 25 basis points to occur prior to the meeting on December 8, 2027 (84.4%). Meanwhile, at the end of 2026, market participants see a 17.1% probability that the rate will, in fact, go up by 25 basis points (to 4.00%).

Previously, it has been repeatedly emphasized that continued rate cuts by the Fed are one of the key negative triggers for a decline in BDC yields. The financial statements of many companies demonstrate NII’s high sensitivity to changes in interest rates, given that the BDC business model is built on issuing non-bank loans, frequently linked to the floating SOFR rate. From September 1, 2025, to March 23, 2026, SOFR has fallen from 4.39% to 3.63%. That automatically narrows the spread between short-term and long-term rates, lowering the profitability of BDCs, since they raise funds at lower rates while issuing loans at higher rates.

Thanks to the reduced likelihood of a Fed rate cut, the SOFR may rebound, further boosting BDCs’ NII. In the longer term, this could backfire in terms of loan portfolio quality management, however, since many borrowers are hoping for lower borrowing rates. This is because the higher the rates, the more difficult it will be to repay debts, potentially increasing the proportion of non-accruals. That is why, I believe, with the current deterioration in the BDC market environment, with problems arising not from NII and interest rates but from borrowers’ solvency, an easing of the Fed’s monetary policy will become a major driver of rising defaults, hurting NAV.

A potential return by the Fed to a rate-hiking cycle would reduce the financial cushion for BDC borrowers, affecting primarily companies with a high proportion of PIK income. As an example, PIK income accounts for 15.50% of blue-chip Ares Capital (AREC) and 12.40% of FSK—numbers that signal their borrowers are unable to service their debt through operating cash flow. It is also important to consider the lag effect, due to which even a belated Fed rate cut (in Q3 2027) will cause many borrowers to deplete their liquidity buffers. That could lead to write-offs of bad debt, hitting BDCs' returns even harder through a drop in NAV than a decline in NII. Furthermore, debtors' solvency is reflected not only in how much cash BDCs can generate but also in the fact that, as a result of inflationary risks, costs for companies themselves related to production, wages, logistics, and energy are rising. In the end, lenders are dealing with two problems: potential increases in loan payments due to SOFR and a drop in operating margins.

Similarities and Differences Between BIZD and PBDC

This selection of funds is based on the fact that they are key holdings of ETFs that invest in BDCs. With non-bank lending companies offering investors double-digit returns, funds can also boast high payouts. That said, these funds have high fees, amounting to 12.86% and 13.49%, respectively. This includes AFFE (indirect expenses of the BDCs themselves that automatically lower the portfolio’s NAV), which means the real base fee for investors is 0.4% and 0.75%.

Even though BIZD is required to purchase “all top-tier assets” (its portfolio managers construct the portfolio based on tracking the MVIS US Business Development Companies Index), because its methodology is tied to market capitalization, top holdings in the portfolios of both funds are virtually identical. It shows that PBDC’s active management has, as a matter of fact, become a "hidden index tracking," that still fails to protect investors from the systemic risk of the BDC market. The question arises as to whether it is necessary to pay a higher fee for this type of management, given that all eight of BDC BIZD’s top holdings are also among PBDC’s top holdings (illustrating the high overlap between the funds’ portfolios).

Based on the fund's website, the portfolio is made up of 53.59% investments in companies with a market capitalization of over $5 billion, while 26.72% consists of investments in companies valued between $1 billion and $5 billion, and the remaining 19.70% consists of investments in smaller BDCs valued at less than $1 billion. BIZD's portfolio comprises 35 holdings, out of which the top 10 account for 78.90% of the total. A portion of the assets, representing 37.26%, are concentrated in cash and commercial paper (for rebalancing purposes).

PDBC portfolio managers’ strategy boils down to evaluating assets based on the quality of the loan portfolio and the cost of borrowed capital. Assets are allocated at the discretion of the portfolio managers. Overvalued assets are reduced in the portfolio, while undervalued assets are increased when there is an opportunity to generate "alpha." Nevertheless, assets that are more undervalued are often small- or mid-cap BDCs, and this automatically entails higher risks in the form of a rising share of non-accruals. In total, a PBDC portfolio consists of 23 holdings, of which the top 10 account for 70.27%.

Active management of the PBDC results in a negligible difference compared to the BIZD in terms of total return and dividend yield, since the approach to portfolio construction for the former ETF is similar to "hidden indexing." Looking at total returns over the past year, results for PBDC are 2.55% higher, although both funds are in the red. Total returns for PBDC and BIZD in 2026 are negative, at -10.98% and -10.51%, respectively.

Analyzing the dividend policy, it is clear that BIZD’s yield is 1.25% higher, although the dividends themselves are paid quarterly. This difference is insignificant, while the dynamics of the payments themselves are the opposite. PBDC increased its dividends for Q3 and Q4 2025 (from $0.79 to $0.83), but BIZD has been gradually reducing its dividends since Q4 2023 (from $0.47 to $0.40).

Summary results of the comparison between BIZD and PBDC are shown in the table below.

Risks Associated with Investments in BIZD and PBDC: A Reason to "Sell"

Given that I believe the current market conditions in the BDC sector are accompanied by deteriorating operating conditions and increased risks, my previous "Hold" recommendation for BIZD and PBDC has been revised to "Sell." There is no reason to bet on the broader market, since the best solution is to selectively target individual companies that demonstrate more reliable results in portfolio quality management. Many BDCs may turn out to be a yield trap after the cycle peaks, since there is already a downward trend in NII and dividend payments. I used to be very interested in BIZD and PBDC because they offered double-digit returns, yet the risk profile is becoming more significant, which offsets this benefit. As an example, while selecting individual companies, Capital Southwest (CSWC) recently became another favorite of mine, given its competitive advantages for further growth potential. Its portfolio consists of 90% 1st Lien Senior Secured Debt, enabling the company to mitigate potential risks associated with defaults.

The reason I’m selling BIZD and PBDC is that the broader BDC market faces greater risks of future defaults, with lenders’ buffers eroding amid rising inflationary risks. Consequently, non-performing loans are already on the rise at some major BDCs, confirmed by FSK (which I recently downgraded to a "Sell" recommendation). In addition, there is an increased likelihood of an economic recession due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. In fact, data from Moody's indicates that the probability of a recession in the U.S. economy has risen to 48.6%. In this regard, one should bear in mind that since World War II, all systemic economic crises have been preceded by a rise in oil prices of more than 50% (with the exception of the COVID-19 crisis).

Conclusion

My comparison of BIZD and PBDC has not convinced me of the need to keep these assets in the portfolio, and I am therefore downgrading my recommendation to "Sell." Currently, a bet on such ETFs is a bet that the U.S. economy will remain stable, which is not to be expected at this time. It is likely that interest rates will remain high, increasing pressure on borrowers and raising the risk of new defaults. The two main pillars of my reasoning for this decision are the downward trend in NII (which is falling instead of rising) and the deterioration in the quality of the loan portfolio (in which the share of non-accruals is only growing).

data.title: The U.S. Labor Market Confounds With Its Countercurrents; It May Be The New Normal Labor Market
data.description:
On one side, there are the tech layoff announcements, often to clean up what tech CEOs widely called “over-hiring” to describe what they’d done in 2020-2022, when their payrolls exploded; Block CEO Jack Dorsey was the latest on X, “yes we over-hired during covid…,” after Block announced that it would lay off nearly half its staff, which had more than tripled in 2020-2022 (from 3,900 to 12,500).

At the same time, there is helter-skelter hiring for AI-related jobs with huge compensation offers leading to what’s locally called a “mansion shortage” in AI epicenter San Francisco.

And there is very tepid hiring in other parts of the private sector, accompanied by massive layoffs at the federal government, and job reductions at state governments.

These currents come amid a crackdown on illegal immigration and the tightening up of some legal immigration that have led to a drop in the total supply of labor.

This is not a labor market the US is used to. It’s a labor market of low unemployment despite low job creation in the private sector and job destruction at the federal government.

Job creation in the private sector is low because there is less demand for labor in some industries, including because AI is being deployed to do things that college graduates entering the labor market would have done, making this job market very difficult to enter for recent graduates.

And job creation is also low because of labor shortages in other industries, such as skilled labor in the trades, including those needed in construction, which has started a whole discussion of how it would have been better for some young people, instead of going to college and loading up on student loans, to go to trade school and then train to be auto technicians or electricians or HVAC technicians or carpenters or welders.

The latest piece of the puzzle in these countercurrents: The four-week average of initial applications for unemployment insurance benefits in the week through Saturday ticked down to 210,500, according to the Labor Department today.

There were only a few weeks in the decades from the 1970s till the pandemic when the four-week average was even lower.

What this shows is that, despite all these announcements, relatively few employees in the US are actually getting laid off without already having another job in the same company or elsewhere lined up:

These historically low figures track freshly discharged people who filed for unemployment insurance compensation at their state unemployment agencies. The agencies then report them to the US Department of Labor by the weekly deadline, which then publishes it. This is not survey-based.

And here is another piece of the puzzle: The four-week average of continued weekly claims for unemployment insurance benefits fell to 1.847 million, according to the Labor Department today.

Over the past five decades, it’s only during the tight labor market in 2018 and 2019 and in the years of the labor shortages from 2022 through early 2023, that the level was ever lower.

This reflects the total number of people who’d initially applied for unemployment insurance at least a week earlier and are still claiming unemployment insurance because they still haven’t found a job.

It shows that overall, private-sector employers are hanging on to their workers despite some global layoff announcements at some big companies, though these announced layoffs don’t all happen in the US, and some don’t lead to actual layoffs, but elimination of “roles” with workers shifting to different jobs in the same company. And some of these layoffs hit remote jobs, while workers for in-office jobs are getting hired.

The convoluted strength of this labor market amid the crackdown on illegal immigration and the drop in the supply of labor shows up in the prime-age labor force participation rate (25-to-54-year-olds), which has been running at 25-year highs, lower only than during the extraordinary period of the Dotcom Bubble (data via the BLS on March 6).

The prime-age labor force participation rate eliminates the issue of the retiring boomers. The overall labor force participation rate shows the percentage of the population that either has a job or is looking for a job. When people retire, they’re no longer “participating” in the labor force but remain in the population until they die. The surge of boomer retirements, which started about 15 years ago, has pushed down the overall labor force participation rate, as these retired boomers are still in the population but no longer “participating” in the labor force.

The Fed has also struggled in dealing with this labor market. And Powell expressed those struggles during the last FOMC press conference. It’s not a weak labor market in the typical US sense with big layoffs and a rise in unemployment; and it’s not a strong labor market in the typical US sense with lots of job creation either. But it may be the new normal labor market.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Buy Disney When Valuation Is Low And Crude Oil High (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have studied and traded The Walt Disney Co. (DIS) since I started investing in 1986. This film and vacation juggernaut is one of the top names in the world of entertainment. Generally, periods of recession and rising oil prices (negatively affecting disposable incomes for consumers and expenses to pay for a Disney trip) have been some of the worst times to own the stock. This condition may describe the first half of 2026 rather aptly.

However, after the dust settles regarding a drop in entertainment spending by Americans and travelers around the globe (with reactions to Trump tariffs already hurting travel to the U.S.), buying Disney stock has proven a terrific idea for longer-term investors. Even better, if you can purchase shares with a stronger-than-average valuation (which may be true in the months ahead), smart investors may be rigging the odds in their favor for nice portfolio gains several years into the future.

To me, the developing economic and stock valuation situation reminds me of the 1990 Persian Gulf War recession plus the late 2008 peak in crude oil and Great Recession span. If this reflection proves correct, acquiring DIS shares under $95, possibly falling back to $80 or lower in the months ahead, may be the most productive course of action for those with a holding period target of 3-5 years or longer.

After being correctly bearish on Disney stock from $110-120 a year ago, I am upgrading my 12-month view to "Buy," with "Strong Buy" territory pegged around $80. Let's review some of my logic.

Buy Disney After Oil Price Spike?

If you can purchase DIS when Wall Street is fretting over a spike in oil and its trickle-down headwind on vacation spending, positive investment returns tend to be your reward. Let's say we get a jump in crude oil to $150 per barrel into late spring on President Trump's Iran war decision. I can all but guarantee conventional wisdom and analyst opinions will turn decidedly bearish on Disney. We could even see prices plunge to multi-year lows around $80.

If this scenario plays out, the ratio of DIS pricing around $80 with crude oil around $150 (nearing 0.5x) would be quite bullish, in my view. Why? Because a multi-year trend of falling crude oil could goose consumer spending on entertainment in future years. Buying near panic-selling lows, just before improving sales and earnings arrive at Disney, is a once-every-decade type opportunity.

Below is a graph of this ratio, with both energy commodities and equities rising over the years from steady Federal Reserve money printing. You will notice the extreme levels of 3x to 4x occurred in the late 1990s, 2016-17, and late 2020 into 2021. Each period proved an astute time to exit or lighten up Disney shareholdings. (I was decidedly bearish on the stock during 2021 around $175 each.)

Conversely, when oil has been high and DIS low, giving us a ratio well under the multi-decade median yearly average of 1.1x, the bullish odds tend to increase for ownership success. As of this writing, crude oil is sitting at $95, with Disney at roughly the same price for a 1:1 par reading.

I have circled in gold extended periods of this ratio approaching/under 0.5x, including a recession in U.S. GDP overall. Over the past 35 years, only 1990 and 2008 fit these criteria best. (And, if short-term pain truly sets the stage for long-term gains, a $200 oil price to $70 DIS quote might open a screaming buy opportunity.)

Historically speaking, measured from both peaks in crude oil, owning DIS over the next 5 years delivered superb total returns, ignoring all other investment factors. Including dividends, Disney shares widely outperformed the S&P 500 index between October 1990 and March 1995, then again between July 2008 and December 2013. Fortunately, both spans included cheap DIS valuation support, similar to today.

Slowly Improving Valuation Backstop

The single financial analysis metric that best explains the bullish valuation of Disney is price-to-book value. Today's 1.57x ratio has only been dramatically lower during the Great Recession in 2009 and several months in 2002 after the dot-com technology bust. With a 30-year median yearly average closer to 2.5x, plenty of upside potential is building in DIS.

The negative material operating events of 2019-20 created a collapse in cash flow vs. interest expense. 2016 saw the most conservative balance in decades, with very little debt before the Fox asset acquisition and appearance of COVID-related travel spending contraction. The 35x ratio of cash flow to total interest expense a decade ago fell to 3x in 2021. A gradual rebound to 11x to 12x in 2025 has returned debt levels to a more normal setting vs. income.

When we include debt and equity values, the enterprise valuation metric has become cheaper and cheaper on net cash EBITDA numbers. The forward 10x EV to EBITDA ratio is the least expensive since early 2019 (pre-Fox).

EV to sales is approaching bargain levels around 2.2x. You have to go back to 2012 to find a less expensive upfront investment setup on revenue.

More good news: the basic earnings yield above 7% currently is the best since 2019. And, if share pricing dips closer to $80 in 2026, we could witness the strongest trailing earnings yield since the end of the 2009 Great Recession.

Final Thoughts

The ill-timed 2019 Fox acquisition with a mountain of debt added, followed by the operating hit of COVID shutdowns at theaters and parks in 2020, has generated one of the worst spans to own Disney in history. In fact, the painstaking recovery from COVID effects has translated into a stock quote actually lower than a decade ago! It goes without saying, shares have sharply underperformed the blue-chip S&P 500 index over this span. Does this mean a more constructive trend relative to U.S. stocks is about to begin? Possibly.

As a note of caution, I will say immediate recession risk (from tariffs and an oil cost explosion higher) could be a heavy weight on the share quote over the next 3-6 months. If spiking crude oil does trip the economy into a contraction, it will be difficult for Disney to rise beyond $100 for the remainder of 2026. However, this investment result may be the same as or even outperform other U.S. equities (like late 2008 throughout 2009).

Below is a table of current Wall Street analyst estimates for 2026-28. Disney is expected to outline sales growth similar to nominal GDP rates in the 5% range, with cost controls, product price increases, and lower net debt (hopefully) growing per-share profits by 10% or higher each year.

If you can purchase DIS closer to a P/E of 10x, you may be able to really stack future investment returns in your favor. Given a recession with stagnant earnings in the $7 area for 2027-28, any price under $80 would be a load-up-the-truck moment for this leading brand-name consumer entertainment business (in my opinion).

My position in Disney is quite small so far, mostly acquired in recent weeks under $100. I am patiently waiting for another stock market selloff on yet higher oil prices and the realization by Wall Street that a recession has begun.

My trading plan is to increase position sizing on price weakness in DIS. I cannot guarantee oil prices will jump again or Disney shares will continue their recent dip. However, if this scenario plays out, I will likely own a considerable stake in the company by early summer.

Of course, I continue to believe Disney can make some operating moves to position itself for even higher earnings in the future. I suggest new CEO Josh D'Amaro seriously look into buying out Roku (ROKU) to control the world's primary gateway to streaming all kinds of content into the home (not just Disney-owned channels/media).

I have upgraded my Disney outlook from "Sell" to "Buy" as an official rating. I was a timid buyer around $95 per share in late March. Aggressive buying and sizing inside my portfolio is a goal at lower share quotes. At this point, I plan on doubling my stake at around $90, with the potential to double again closer to $80 (if such a move occurs).

For an upside forecast, I am targeting $120 for the second half of 2027, using a 17x P/E on EPS of $7.00. Followed by $150 in late 2028, given earnings of $8 per share and a P/E closer to 19x. If debt is reduced in future years, while Disney has averaged a P/E above 20x over the past three decades, a reversion-to-the-mean move could support far higher DIS quotes. Remember, I could be a little early in this call for rising Disney quotes.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Pulls Back
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 1.7 percentage points to 32.1%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the sixth consecutive week.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 0.5 percentage points to 18.1%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 88th time in 90 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 2.2 percentage points to 49.8%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the seventh consecutive week.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 3.9 percentage points to –17.7%. The bull-bear spread is unusually low and is below its historical average of 6.5% for the seventh consecutive week.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what they thought about the Federal Reserve’s decision to keep interest rates unchanged.

Here is how they responded:

  • They should have raised rates: 13.7%
  • It was the right move: 66.5%
  • They should have cut rates: 14.2%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 5.6%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 32.1%, up 1.7 percentage points
  • Neutral: 18.1%, up 0.5 percentage points
  • Bearish: 49.8%, down 2.2 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Mettler-Toledo International's Growth Is Great, But It's Too Early For An Upgrade
data.description:
One company that I have been bearish about for over a year now is Mettler-Toledo International Inc. (MTD). Even though the company continues to see an increase in revenue, profits, and cash flows, my issue with it has been primarily because of its lofty valuation. So far, that bearishness has paid off. Since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘sell’ candidate back in October of last year, shares are down 7.3% while the market is flat. And since I originally rated it a ‘sell’ in January of that year, the stock has fallen 10.4%. Over that same window of time, the S&P 500 is up 11%.

From a purely fundamental standpoint, the company is actually doing well right now. Yes, there have been some mixed financial figures. But on the whole, it is growing. I would say, based on where we are, it is getting closer to an upgrade to a ‘hold.’ But I'm not ready to pull the trigger on that just yet. I would like to see the stock fall another 10% or so at least or see a meaningful improvement in profitability before upgrading it.

Taking A Fresh Look At Mettler-Toledo International

Operationally, Mettler-Toledo International strikes me as an intriguing business. Management describes the enterprise as a supplier of precision instruments and services. It is a truly global company, with only 42% of revenue coming from North America and South America combined last year. Another 29% comes from Europe, with the rest coming from Asia and various countries across the globe. In total, the company sells its products in more than 140 countries, and it has a direct presence in around 40 of those. To really understand the company, though, we need to dig a bit deeper. And this involves touching briefly on each of its operating segments and the markets that it serves.

Management specifies in their annual report the wide array of solutions that they make available for customers. For instance, around 56% of revenue last year came from laboratory instruments. Examples here would include balances, pipettes, analytical instruments, laboratory-oriented software such as its LabX platform, certain automated chemistry solutions, and even process analytics services. The firm also sells industrial instruments that are used in not only the pharmaceutical and chemical industries but also when it comes to food, discrete manufacturing, and more. Under this category, the company provides not only software but also certain devices such as X-ray systems, metal detection systems, check weighing systems, and more. All told, industrial solutions accounted for 39% of revenue last year. The final 5% of sales came from retail weighing solutions that are made available to supermarkets and other food-oriented enterprises. Specifically, I'm talking about weighing and software solutions.

Instead of breaking up data in a more granular way based on these types of product categories, the firm actually organizes its segments based on geography. These would be its operations in the US, in Switzerland, in Western Europe, in China, and then in all other miscellaneous parts of the world combined. This means that the company has five different operating segments based on geography, which does make understanding the health of different parts of the world easier than with some other companies. But to be honest with you, I wish that the firm focused more on the actual types of products and services that it offers. But I digress.

The newest data that investors have right now covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue during that time was $1.13 billion. That was up a bit from $1.05 billion reported a year earlier. As revenue grew, profitability increased also. Net income rose from $252.3 million to $285.8 million. It is true that operating cash flow declined from $266.2 million to $225.8 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $289.4 million to $333.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded slightly from $355.2 million to $367.8 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and half of the cash flow metrics that I looked at for the company ended up increasing year over year. On the whole, this is positive. But it is important to keep in mind that not all parts of the company behaved similarly. Take its US operations as an example. Revenue for this unit did climb from $1.58 billion to $1.65 billion year over year. Management chalked this up to an increase in revenue associated with process analytics, food retail, and product inspection categories. But while revenue did rise for 2025 as a whole, overall segment profits dropped from $393.1 million to $375.4 million. Some of this was unavoidable. What I mean by this is that the company saw lower sales volume because, in 2025, the firm saw a delay in shipping some of its goods. That negatively impacted sales by 1% for the year and had an impact on profitability as a result. But then on top of this, the business was impaired by higher tariff costs.

Its Swiss operations performed considerably better by comparison. Year over year, revenue grew from $1.02 billion to $1.05 billion. However, this is a bit of a mirage. The vast majority of sales for this segment actually come from making the products produced by the segment available to the other operating segments of the enterprise. Actual sales to external customers dropped around 4%, with management chalking this up to the aforementioned shipping delays that the company dealt with. Regardless, I am happy that overall profits inched up from $280.6 million to $283.7 million.

In Western Europe, we saw segment revenue climb from $1.04 billion to $1.10 billion. This stemmed from growth associated with not only its existing segments but also external customers, with the latter attributable to robust demand in the core industrial and retail product categories. The higher revenue, combined with certain cost-cutting initiatives that the company has been working on, pushed segment profits up considerably from $206.3 million to $226.7 million. This is a fantastic increase, especially considering that it is in large part the result of margin expansion initiatives that the company has clearly seen some benefit from.

In China, revenue grew from $948.6 million to $964.5 million. This was driven by higher sales to external customers and to the other operating segments of the business. This was in spite of the fact that the company dealt with shipping delays and was thanks to slightly stronger demand when it came to laboratory and industrial products. Unfortunately, however, segment profits dipped from $345.8 million to $344.9 million. Even though the company achieved success from certain margin expansion initiatives and benefited from higher sales, tariff costs proved to be a problem.

The most remarkable growth that the company exhibited during this time came from its other operations, which include its business activities in Southeast Asia, Latin America, Eastern Europe, and miscellaneous other nations. Revenue surged from $759.6 million to $829.4 million. Even if we strip out sales to other operating segments, we get a rise in revenue of 6.9% year over year. That is superior to what we have seen with any of its other segments. And according to management, this was thanks to growth across most of its product categories, with process analytics proving to be a particular bright spot for the company.

When it comes to 2026, management mentioned that constant currency revenue is likely to grow by around 4%. And if we take the midpoint of profitability estimates for the year, that would mean net profits of around $958.7 million. This would likely mean adjusted operating cash flow of $1.13 billion and EBITDA of $1.34 billion. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart below how shares of the company are priced compared to how other companies that have some similarities to it are priced. In addition to the stock looking very expensive right now on an absolute basis, it's also a bit toward the pricey end of the spectrum compared to these other enterprises. For instance, it is more expensive than three of the five comparable firms using both the price-to-earnings approach and the EV-to-EBITDA approach. And when it comes to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach, it is more costly than four of the companies.

Because of how expensive the stock is, I cannot in good faith be anything other than slightly bearish about it. But this doesn't mean that I am pessimistic about it from an operational standpoint. Management has a solid track record of growth, both by means of acquisitions and through innovation. During 2025, for instance, the company doled out $93.8 million in cash for other companies that it bought up. Plus, it could be on the hook for another $35.5 million of contingent consideration associated with these purchases. And when it comes to innovation, the company has had some noteworthy successes. This includes X-ray inspection technology for the packaging activities, its AnyLevel tank weighing sensors, EVA titrators, compact automation weighing indicators, and more. In the future, I expect continued investments to be made along these lines, but that doesn't change the fact that shares look very pricey at the moment.

Takeaway

At its core, I think that Mettler-Toledo International is a really interesting business. But in all honesty, it's not one that I can get behind from an investment perspective right now. The stock has underperformed the market as I predicted that it would. And even though shares are actually more attractive now from a valuation standpoint than they were then, they are still not cheap enough to justify anything other than a slightly bearish rating. That's why I have decided to maintain it as a ‘sell’ candidate at this time.

data.title: Meta Platforms Down 7%, Google Breaks Key Support - What Comes Next For The Tech Heavyweights?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The technology sector faced a turbulent trading session as two of its largest titans, Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (Google)(GOOGL)(GOOG), saw their share prices under pressure.

A combination of legal setbacks, internal restructuring, and shifting investor sentiment toward "AI fatigue" weighed heavily on their performance.

The Nasdaq 100 index was down around 1.1% at the time of writing, with Meta Platforms trading down around 7% on the day.

Overall sentiment has taken a hit today on fears that the US-Iran war will not come to an end as the two sides appear some ways off from a deal.

This has weighed on market sentiment as oil prices are also up around 5%.

However, both Meta and Google are also facing their own challenges. Let us take a look at these challenges individually and see what the technical picture for both companies tells us.

Meta Platforms: Legal blows and workforce reshuffling

META is under pressure, which is largely being fueled by a landmark legal verdict and news of further internal job cuts.

The Social Media Addiction Verdict: A jury found Meta (and YouTube) liable in a high-profile case involving social media addiction. The jury ruled that Meta’s platform designs—specifically Instagram—contributed to harmful, addictive behavior in minors and that the company failed to warn users of these risks. Meta was assigned 70% of the responsibility, resulting in $6 million in damages ($3 million compensatory and $3 million punitive). While the dollar amount is nominal for a company of Meta's size, investors fear the legal precedent could open the floodgates for thousands of similar pending lawsuits.

Ongoing Layoffs: Meta confirmed it is laying off several hundred more employees across divisions, including Facebook, Instagram, Reality Labs, and its sales units. While CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s "Year of Efficiency" was initially cheered by Wall Street in 2023-2024, the continued cuts in 2026 suggest a more painful transition as the company redirects capital away from traditional social media operations toward intensive AI development.

Meta Platforms Daily Chart, March 26, 2026

The technical outlook for META has shifted from a period of consolidation into a distinctly bearish phase as the stock has broken the November 2025 swing low.

This leaves META trading at levels last seen in April/May 2025.

The stock price is now trading below all the key moving averages, like the 50, 100, and 200 SMAs.

SMA 200 (Dark Blue - $688.53): The stock is now trading well below its long-term trendline. This often signals a transition from a bull market to a structural downtrend.

SMA 50 (Orange - $645.54) & SMA 100 (Purple - $643.02): The price failed to find support at these intermediate levels. Crucially, the 50-day SMA is beginning to curl downward, suggesting that short-term momentum is now firmly in favor of the bears.

The stock is currently testing a psychological support zone around the $560–$580 mark. If it fails to hold here, the next major historical support sits near the $520–$540 level (the lows seen in May 2025).

Any relief rally will face heavy "confluence" resistance at $645, where the 50- and 100-day SMAs converge. This area, which acted as support in February, will now likely act as a "ceiling" for the price.

Alphabet: Bear market whispers and spending anxiety

Alphabet’s stock performance was similarly sluggish, with shares hovering near $289.59. Despite beating earnings estimates earlier in the quarter, the stock has struggled to maintain momentum, sliding toward what technical analysts are calling "bear market territory."

AI Capex Concerns: The primary weight on Alphabet is the staggering cost of its AI infrastructure. Investors are growing wary of the "Capex Trap"—the massive capital expenditure required to stay competitive in generative AI without a clear, immediate timeline for a massive return on investment.

Institutional Selling: SEC filings revealed that several institutional players, including Threadgill Financial LLC, have begun trimming their positions. Threadgill cut its Alphabet holdings by nearly 35%, a move that mirrors a broader trend of "profit-taking" as funds rotate out of Big Tech and into energy or value stocks.

Secondary Legal Pressure: Like Meta, Alphabet’s YouTube was also named in the addiction verdict (assigned 30% liability). This adds to Alphabet’s existing mountain of antitrust challenges globally, creating a "regulatory overhang" that limits the stock's multiple.

Alphabet (Google) Daily Chart, March 26, 2026

The technical picture for Google is quite similar, with the stock now trading at November 2025 levels.

The stock remains in a long-term uptrend relative to its 200-day average; the short- to medium-term outlook has deteriorated sharply following a failure at key resistance levels.

The price has decisively fallen below both the 50- and 100-day MAs. In early March, the 100-day SMA acted as support, but today’s aggressive sell-off has turned these moving averages into major overhead resistance.

The 200-day MA is the ultimate "line in the sand." While the stock is still above this long-term trendline, the trajectory is sloping toward it. A drop to this level would represent a 10% correction from current prices.

The stock is approaching the $280–$285 zone. This was a previous consolidation area in late 2025. If this fails to hold, the next stop is the 200-day SMA near $262.

Any recovery attempts will likely be capped at the $310–$315 level. The stock would need to reclaim this zone on high volume to negate the current bearish thesis.

The road ahead

Moving forward, the immediate future of global stocks in the short term will hinge on the Middle East. If oil prices remain elevated, markets could remain cautious, and risk-off sentiment may prevail. This would not bode well for stocks as a whole.

Earlier today, we heard from JPMorgan, who said investors are shifting back to cash, similar to 2022. This is a sign of the uncertainty prevalent at the minute, and the situation continues to fluctuate on a daily basis as news filters through.

Short-term trading may be the best option at the minute with the broader picture unclear.
